evidence: The paper measures importance as "the average increase in (worsening of) the PMC that results from the replacement of the value of a variable by a randomly chosen value," which is the definition of permutation importance, not impurity importance. Permutation importance is robust to impurity-bias artifacts. Although the paper extensively interprets the ranking (age most important, followed by income and socioeconomic position), it avoids the bias because permutation importance does not magnify effect sizes super-linearly or misattribute interaction effects.
